title: dnaA at 8.6th percentile of v2ecoli proteome
caption: dnaA's ParCa-cached translation efficiency sits at the bottom 9% of the proteome — biologically improbable for a master regulator.
simulations: No simulation. Read directly from the ParCa-fitted parameters cache (out/cache/sim_data.cPickle) — specifically translation_efficiencies_by_monomer for every protein, then ranks dnaA's value against the full proteome. One-shot analysis.
interpretation: DnaA is a master regulator that needs hundreds of copies per cell, yet ParCa places its translation efficiency in the slowest 9% of all proteins. This is the root-cause finding for the 5× count shortfall seen in chart 01: even without any homeostasis effects, the ParCa fit can't physically deliver enough DnaA at default TE. Either ParCa's fit is mis-anchored for this gene (e.g. the wrong fold-change reference in the proteomics input) or the upstream mass-spec count is genuinely an over-estimate that the model is correctly rejecting. Expert input on which is more plausible would resolve dnaa-01's primary calibration gate.
